topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat accusingly on the mahogany desk. I picked it up with the tips of my fingers, the paper feeling cheap against my skin. It was a letter, penned in a florid, almost theatrical hand, apologizing for something that had happened nearly a decade ago. A decade. The sheer audacity of the timing made my jaw tighten. I unfolded the page slowly, the sound of the tearing paper echoing too loudly in the otherwise silent study. Reading it was an exercise in pure, unadulterated annoyance. Every carefully chosen adjective, every clumsy attempt at remorse, felt like a deliberate insult to my memory of the event. I let the letter drop back onto the blotter with a soft thud, a barely perceptible twitch in my lip betraying the utter lack of feeling I was experiencing.

[story 2]

When the courier arrived, I didn't look up from the financial reports spread across my desk. My posture remained rigidly straight, every muscle coiled tight beneath my tailored suit. The letter was presented with a mumbled formality that grated on my nerves. I took the parchment, my gaze sweeping over the elegant script as if cataloging flaws in a poorly constructed piece of machinery. Inside, the words were saccharine, dripping with regret that felt utterly hollow. I reread a particularly flowery sentence twice, my eyes narrowing until they were slits. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, a sound devoid of warmth. I folded the letter once, precisely, and slid it into a drawer, the metallic scrape of the drawer closing sounding like a final dismissal.

[story 3]

I found the letter tucked inside a forgotten box of old correspondence. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon light illuminating the aged paper. It was from him, years after the regrettable exchange. I didn't rush to read it. Instead, I circled the box slowly, my movements economical and stiff. When I finally broke the seal, the contents seemed offensively sentimental. My brow furrowed, not in sympathy, but in a kind of profound distaste for the effort expended. I read through it, my expression unchanging, a mask of cool indifference. When I finished, I placed the letter face down on the shelf, a deliberate act of shelving it away, as if the contents were contaminated.
